fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out a bitmask flag toolkit that our platform teams keep reinventing every quarter. The core idea is simple: developers declare a named set of on/off options backed by an integer, and the library handles all the combining, querying, and printing so nobody has to hand-roll bit math anymore. We've had a few incidents where unknown bits sneaking through caused silent data corruption downstream, so there needs to be a clear story around how 'stray' bits are handled — some callers want them rejected, some want them silently stripped, some want to keep them. Also refer to how we handled the 'external catch-all' pattern in the options registry module, that same concept applies here for sets that need to cover every possible bit. The toolkit should support the usual set algebra people expect (combine, subtract, overlap checks), plus the ability to iterate which named flags are active in a value, serialize/deserialize to a human-readable pipe-separated format, and a classify/dispatch helper for routing values through labeled buckets. There's also a non-ASCII flag name situation that came up from the i18n team — make sure that works. Output formatting for the adapter layer needs to be consistent across all operations. Let's get this scoped out properly.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for unknown bits, there are three construction policies and they should behave pretty literally. 'checked' rejects any integer that contains bits outside the declared flags and returns an absent/none result, 'masked' silently drops anything outside the known flags, and 'raw'/'retain' keeps the integer exactly as-is including unknown bits. The set’s known bits are just the union of all declared flag bit patterns. That matters for the algebra too: union, difference, and intersection should preserve whatever unknown bits are already in the operands, but complement is always masked to known bits and never creates unknown bits.

Also, for the catch-all behavior, the flag is declared with an empty string as its name and 255 as its bit pattern. Since known bits are the union of declared flag bit patterns, any set that includes that catch-all ends up with known bits = 255, so every possible 8-bit value is treated as known. That catch-all should not come back through name lookup though, so lookup by empty string returns none. If it shows up in catalog/decompose_named output, it should appear exactly as 'name= bits=255'. The two catalog entries that use this are 'external' with A=1, B=2, C=4, ABC=7, catch-all=255, and 'external_full' with only the catch-all=255. This same detail should be reflected in the Flag Set Catalog section of start.md for 'external' and 'external_full', where the empty-name flag ('') with bits=255 means all 256 possible 8-bit values are known.

On decomposition, the order is declaration order, and that is what decides how combined flags behave. A flag is yielded when all of its bits are present in the remaining unaccounted bits, and once yielded, those bits get removed from the remaining pool. So if ABC=7 is declared before A=1, B=2, C=4, then ABC comes out first and the individual parts do not. If A=1, B=2, C=4 are declared first, then those come out individually and ABC is skipped. Any leftover bits that do not match a named flag should come out as one trailing element in decompose, but not in decompose_named. Also, ZERO=0 should never be yielded.

For text in and out, the delimiter is exactly ' | '. Parsing should split on '|' and trim whitespace on each token. Tokens can be exact, case-sensitive flag names or '0x'-prefixed hex literals. The empty string means bits=0. The three parse variants are also intentionally different: 'parse' keeps unknown hex bits, 'parse_masked' drops hex bits outside known flags, and 'parse_named' rejects any hex token with error=invalid_hex_flag. If the token is a flag name we do not recognize, that should always be error=unrecognized_named_flag. On the way back out, 'to_text' emits named flags in declaration order and then adds any leftover bits as a hex literal, 'to_text_masked' drops unknown bits and never emits a hex literal, and 'to_text_named' only emits known flag names. Empty value should serialize to an empty string in every variant.

trigger_keywords: debug format output
display label for set
how to format flag value
set name in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each flag set has a display label used in debug formatting: abc→'Abc', abc_inverted→'AbcInverted', zero→'Zero', zero_one→'ZeroOne', unicode→'Unicode', empty→'Empty', overlapping→'Overlapping', overlapping_full→'OverlappingFull', external→'External', external_full→'ExternalFull', abcd→'Abcd'. The debug format is: 'DisplayLabel(flag1 | flag2 | 0xNN)' where contained named flags appear in declaration order, leftover unknown bits appear as a hex literal, and the empty value is shown as '0x0'. Numeric formats use the raw underlying integer: upper_hex and lower_hex omit leading zeros and the '0x' prefix (e.g. value 11 → upper 'B', lower 'b'); octal and binary also omit leading zeros.

trigger_keywords: classify operation
pattern matching on flags
dispatch value to label
match arm order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The classify operation takes only a 'value' integer (no 'set' field) and matches it against a hardcoded ordered list of pattern arms. The first arm whose pattern equals the value wins and its label is returned; if no arm matches, a default label is returned. Patterns are computed using flag set algebra (union, intersection, symmetric difference) on the abcd set. The test cases show: value=8 → label='D', value=0 → label='A and B | empty', value=15 → label='All flags'. The arm list is fixed in the implementation and must be defined in declaration order — earlier arms take priority over later ones, which is why zero-producing patterns appear early and catch values that reduce to empty.

trigger_keywords: output format for value
how bits are rendered
hex and binary representation
two digit hex output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A single flag value renders on one line as: 'bits=<decimal> hex=0x<two-lowercase-hex-digits> bin=<eight-binary-digits>'. The hex field is always exactly two lowercase hex digits with '0x' prefix (e.g. 9 → '0x09', 255 → '0xff'). The bin field is always exactly 8 binary digits with leading zeros (e.g. 1 → '00000001'). Optional results prefix with 'outcome=some' on its own line before the value line, or 'outcome=none' alone. Sequence results start with 'count=N' then one value line per element. Query operations use their own formats: is_empty uses 'bits=N is_empty=true/false', contains uses 'lhs=N rhs=N contains=true/false', etc. Errors render as 'error=<category>' with categories: invalid_hex_flag, unrecognized_named_flag.

trigger_keywords: overlapping flags behavior
flags sharing bits
same bit in multiple flags
duplicate bit patterns
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The toolkit supports flags that share bits — this is valid by design. The 'overlapping' set has AB=3 and BC=6 (they share bit 2). The 'overlapping_full' set has A=1, B=1, C=1 (all same bit), D=2. Known bits are still the union of all flag bit patterns. For decomposition, each flag is checked in declaration order against remaining bits; once bits are consumed they are not available for later flags. For to_text_named with overlapping sets, a value of 1 in 'overlapping' emits empty string because bit 1 alone doesn't fully satisfy AB=3 or BC=6 (neither flag's complete bit pattern is present). Catalog lists all declared flags including duplicates/overlaps.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'classify', 'empty', 'all', 'raw_bits', 'from_value_checked', 'from_value_masked', 'from_value_raw', 'lookup_name', 'is_empty', 'is_all', 'contains', 'intersects', 'has_unknown_bits', 'union', 'intersection', 'difference', 'symmetric_difference', 'complement', 'insert', 'remove', 'extend', 'clear_unknown', 'decompose', 'decompose_named', 'union_all', 'catalog', 'compare', 'format', 'to_text', 'parse', 'to_text_masked', 'parse_masked', 'to_text_named', 'parse_named'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The three-policy construction model (checked/masked/raw) for handling unknown bits, corresponding to ops from_value_checked, from_value_masked, and from_value_raw in Feature 1 of start.md

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect handy — could you check the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or the PRD in start.md? The Flag Set Catalog and Core Features sections should cover most of the specifics. If it's about output formatting or a particular operation's edge case, the test JSON files are the ground truth.

feature10_match.json.description: Dispatch a flag value through an ordered list of pattern arms, returning the label of the first arm whose pattern equals the value. Patterns are built from flag combinations using union, intersection, and symmetric-difference; because empty-producing patterns appear early, every value that reduces to empty maps to that early arm. A value matching none of the arms maps to a default label.

feature1_1_empty.json.description: Build the canonical empty flag value for a given flag set and read back its underlying bits. The empty value has no bits set, regardless of which flags the set defines, including sets that have no flags at all or a set whose catch-all flag would otherwise cover every bit.

feature1_2_all.json.description: Build the value containing every bit that belongs to a defined flag in the set, then read its underlying bits. For an ordinary set this is the union of all declared flag bits; for a set whose only members declare no bits it is empty; for a set carrying a catch-all flag that spans every bit it is the all-ones value.

feature1_3_bits.json.description: Construct a flag value from a raw integer while retaining every bit (including bits that do not correspond to any defined flag) and read back the stored bits. The stored bits equal the input exactly.

feature1_4_from_bits.json.description: Validate a raw integer against the set of known flag bits. The result is a present value carrying those bits when every set bit corresponds to a defined flag, or an absent result when any bit falls outside the known flags. A present result reports its bits; an absent result reports that no value was produced.

feature1_5_from_bits_truncate.json.description: Construct a flag value from a raw integer, dropping any bits that do not correspond to a defined flag. The stored bits are the input masked down to the known-flag bits.

feature1_6_from_bits_retain.json.description: Construct a flag value from a raw integer, retaining every bit exactly as supplied even when some bits do not correspond to any defined flag. The stored bits equal the input.

feature1_7_from_name.json.description: Look up a single defined flag by its exact textual name. A match returns the value of that flag; lookup is case-sensitive and does not accept expressions, hex literals, the empty string, or names not declared in the set. A flag declared with an empty name is never returned by name lookup.

feature2_1_is_empty.json.description: Report whether a flag value has no bits set at all. Any non-zero value, including bits that do not correspond to a defined flag, is not empty.

feature2_2_is_all.json.description: Report whether a flag value contains every bit that belongs to a defined flag. Extra bits outside the known flags do not affect the answer; a set with no flags (or whose flags declare no bits) considers the empty value to contain all flags.

feature2_3_contains.json.description: Report whether the first flag value contains all bits of the second. The empty value is contained by everything; a value contains itself; a value does not contain a flag whose bits are not all present, including bits outside the known flags.

feature2_4_intersects.json.description: Report whether two flag values share at least one set bit. The empty value intersects nothing; values sharing any bit (including bits outside the known flags) intersect.

feature2_5_unknown_bits.json.description: Report whether a flag value carries any bit that does not correspond to a defined flag. Values whose bits are all accounted for by known flags report false; values containing stray bits report true.

feature3_1_union.json.description: Combine two flag values, producing a value that has every bit set in either operand. Bits outside the known flags are preserved.

feature3_2_intersection.json.description: Combine two flag values, producing a value that has only the bits set in both operands.

feature3_3_difference.json.description: Remove from the first flag value every bit that is set in the second, keeping the remaining bits of the first (including bits outside the known flags).

feature3_4_symmetric_difference.json.description: Combine two flag values, producing a value that has exactly the bits set in one operand but not the other.

feature3_5_complement.json.description: Produce the complement of a flag value with respect to the known flags: the result contains exactly the known-flag bits that the input lacks, and never contains bits outside the known flags. For a set whose catch-all flag spans every bit, the complement of empty is the all-ones value.

feature4_1_insert.json.description: Add the bits of the second value into the first, returning the updated bits. Inserting is idempotent and preserves bits outside the known flags.

feature4_2_remove.json.description: Clear from the first value every bit present in the second, returning the updated bits. Removing a flag that is absent leaves the value unchanged.

feature4_3_extend.json.description: Grow a flag value by adding all bits of another value into it, returning the updated bits. Extending accumulates bits and preserves bits outside the known flags.

feature4_4_truncate.json.description: Drop from a flag value every bit that does not correspond to a defined flag, returning the remaining known-flag bits.

feature5_1_iter.json.description: Enumerate the contained flags of a value as a sequence of sub-values. Each defined flag whose bits are all present is yielded once in declaration order; any leftover bits that do not correspond to a defined flag are yielded together as a final element. A flag whose value is a combination of finer flags is yielded as a single element only when no finer decomposition applies.

cases.expected_output:
count=3
bits=1 hex=0x01 bin=00000001
bits=2 hex=0x02 bin=00000010
bits=8 hex=0x08 bin=00001000

count=4
bits=1 hex=0x01 bin=00000001
bits=2 hex=0x02 bin=00000010
bits=4 hex=0x04 bin=00000100
bits=248 hex=0xf8 bin=11111000

feature5_2_iter_names.json.description: Enumerate the contained, named flags of a value as a sequence of (name, bits) pairs in declaration order. Bits that do not correspond to a defined flag are not reported. When a value matches a combined flag declared before its components, that combined name is reported instead of the components.

feature5_3_collect.json.description: Fold a sequence of flag values into a single value by taking the union of all of them, returning the combined bits. An empty sequence yields the empty value; bits outside the known flags are preserved.

feature6_flags_metadata.json.description: List the flags declared by a set as (name, bits) pairs in declaration order. Combined flags and a catch-all flag declared with an empty name are included; a set with no declared flags lists nothing.

cases.expected_output:
count=4
name=A bits=1
name=B bits=2
name=C bits=4
name=ABC bits=7

count=5
name=A bits=1
name=B bits=2
name=C bits=4
name=ABC bits=7
name= bits=255

feature7_compare.json.description: Compare two flag values for equality and ordering. Equality holds when both carry the same bits; ordering compares the underlying integer magnitude of the bits.

feature8_format.json.description: Render a flag value in several textual forms: a debug form that prefixes the set name and lists the contained flag names (with any leftover bits shown as a hex literal, and the empty value shown as a zero hex literal), and the bare numeric forms in upper-hex, lower-hex, octal, and binary of the underlying bits.

cases.expected_output:
debug=Abc(A | B | C)
upper_hex=7
lower_hex=7
octal=7
binary=111

debug=External(A | B | 0x8)
upper_hex=B
lower_hex=b
octal=13
binary=1011

feature9_1_to_text.json.description: Serialize a flag value to a bar-separated text form. Contained named flags are written in declaration order joined by ' | '; any leftover bits that do not correspond to a flag are appended as a single '0x'-prefixed hex literal. The empty value serializes to an empty string.

feature9_2_from_text.json.description: Parse a flag value from bar-separated text. Tokens are flag names or '0x'-prefixed hex literals separated by '|', surrounding whitespace is ignored, and the empty string parses to the empty value. Unknown bits supplied as hex are retained. An unrecognized name yields an unrecognized-named-flag error and an unparsable or oversized hex token yields an invalid-hex-flag error.

feature9_3_to_text_truncate.json.description: Serialize a flag value to bar-separated text after first dropping any bits that do not correspond to a flag. Only known flag names appear; there is never a trailing hex literal. The empty result is an empty string.

feature9_4_from_text_truncate.json.description: Parse a flag value from bar-separated text, but drop any bits supplied via hex literals that do not correspond to a flag. Names and whitespace are handled as in the retaining parse; the empty string parses to the empty value.

feature9_5_to_text_strict.json.description: Serialize a flag value to bar-separated text using only known flag names, dropping any bits that do not correspond to a flag and never emitting a hex literal. Values whose only set bits are unknown serialize to an empty string.

feature9_6_from_text_strict.json.description: Parse a flag value from bar-separated text accepting only flag names (and whitespace) — hex literals are rejected outright. Valid input combines the named flags; the empty string parses to the empty value. An unrecognized name yields an unrecognized-named-flag error and any hex token yields an invalid-hex-flag error.
